_comment: [34.86] COMMONWEALTH AIRPLANE REINFORCEMENT AND SQUADRON WITHDRAWAL SCHEDULE -- the chart that makes Malta a place that GETS STRONGER. A FIFTH data file, on the principle data/malta_44.json, data/air_facilities_60_5.json and data/air_establishments.json already set: this is not a rate chart, it is a TWENTY-EIGHT-ROW SCHEDULE with its own key, its own withdrawal column and its own type-to-role reading. Every row is transcribed from the 1979 book: the OCR in docs/rules/90-charts-tables-and-play-aids.md was checked against the scan (PDF page 143), and where the two differ THE SCAN IS THE ARBITER -- every divergence found is listed in _ocr_corrections. ⚠ THE FIRST PASS'S HEADLINE CLAIM ('checked cell by cell... survived everywhere but two glyphs') WAS FALSE AND IS WITHDRAWN, 2026-07-22: a second reading at 8x found FOUR MORE aircraft-name cells where the OCR and this file had both departed from the printed page (1941 May, 1942 May, and two in 1942 Jul), one of them already silently corrected and unrecorded. The row totals, the 6,691 aeroplanes, the WD column and the Key did survive. The names are now read glyph by glyph off 300-dpi crops enlarged 8x, and every one of them resolves to a type printed on the [4.44A] chart (PDF p.112 fighters, p.113 bombers) -- which is the cross-check that was missing the first time: a name that is NOT on [4.44A] is evidence of a misread, not of the book's spelling.
_chart_label: THE BOOK NUMBERS THIS CHART TWICE AND THE TWO DISAGREE. The chart's own printed heading (PDF p.143) reads '[34.84] COMMONWEALTH AIRPLANE REINFORCEMENT AND SQUADRON WITHDRAWAL CHART', while case 34.85 points at it as 'the Commonwealth Airplane Reinforcement and Squadron Withdrawal Schedule (Case 34.86)' and case 34.86 IS that title with a 'see Charts and Tables'. 34.84 is the case that describes how airplane reinforcements arrive, so the heading is the printer carrying the wrong case number down from the paragraph above; the engine calls it [34.86], which is the case that names it. Recorded rather than silently normalised. (docs/rules/90 also prints '[34.86] COMMONWEALTH PILOT ARRIVAL TABLE' over what case 34.88 names -- the same off-by-two, in the same block of the play aids.)
_ocr_corrections: EVERY PLACE THIS FILE DEPARTS FROM docs/rules/90's OCR, WITHOUT EXCEPTION. The list is the file's audit trail, so a correction made but not listed is as much a defect as a correction not made -- which is exactly what happened to the 1941 May row on the first pass.

1941 May (GT 31..34): OCR reads '16xBeaufighter II'. THE SCAN READS '16 x Beaufighter IF' (crop x150-880, y955-1060 of the 300-dpi render of p.143, enlarged 4x -- unambiguous). Transcribed as Beaufighter IF. ⚠ THIS CORRECTION WAS MADE ON THE FIRST PASS AND LEFT UNRECORDED; listed here 2026-07-22.

1942 May (GT 79..82): OCR reads '3xBeaufighter IIF'. THE SCAN READS '3 x Beaufighter IF' (crop x1190-1430, y1300-1345, enlarged 8x). Corrected 2026-07-22 -- this file previously carried the OCR's 'Beaufighter IIF' and mapped it to the Mk. VIF row. There is no Beaufighter Mk. IIF on [4.44A]; the Mk. IF is on it, and is what is printed.
1942 Jul (GT 87..90): OCR reads '4 x Beaufighter II'. THE SCAN READS '4 x Beaufighter IF' (crop x855-1660, y1770-1890, enlarged 4x). Corrected 2026-07-22, same defect as the row above.

1942 Jul (GT 87..90): OCR reads '30 x Hurricane IC'. THE SCAN READS '30 x Hurricane IIC' (crop x1050-1360, y1830-1868, enlarged 7x -- the two I's are plain). Corrected 2026-07-22. There is no Hurricane Mk. IC on [4.44A]; the Mk. IIC is on it, and is what is printed.
1942 Sep (GT 95..98): OCR reads '58 x Hurricane IIIC'. THE SCAN READS 'Hurricane IIC' (there is no Mk. IIIC on the [4.44A] chart, and no such Hurricane). Transcribed as Hurricane IIC.

1942 Nov (GT 103..106): OCR reads 'WD: 2F+'. THE SCAN READS 'WD: 2F*.' -- the asterisk is the chart's own footnote ('One squadron must consist of at least 12 Beaufighters'), and the chart prints no dagger-plus anywhere. Transcribed as 2F*.

1941 Mar (GT 23..26) prints '16 x Wellington' with no mark, and the OCR agrees. Read as Wellington Mk. I: it is the only Wellington on the [4.44A] chart before the Mk. IV first appears in the schedule's own April row, and the Mk. I is what [60.44]-era Commonwealth squadrons flew. A READING, not a correction. Flagged.

AFTER THESE, EVERY `printed` NAME IN THIS FILE IS A TYPE PRINTED ON [4.44A]. tests/test_malta.py::test_the_chart_rows_carry_the_names_the_scan_prints pins the four cells that were wrong and checks that every one of them is on the record here, because the three 2026-07-22 corrections were all found by asking the question the first pass answered wrongly: a name absent from the aircraft chart is a misread until the scan says otherwise.
_book_disagrees_with_itself_january_1941: THE BOOK CONTRADICTS ITSELF ABOUT THE JANUARY 1941 ROW, and the chart is the arbiter (the 54.17 class: recorded beside the applied reading, never resolved silently). Case 34.84's worked example (docs/rules/34-the-aircraft.md) reads: 'E.g., in January of 1941 the Commonwealth Player receives a total of 59 planes (44 HURRICANES, 12 BLENHEIMS, 2 WELLINGTONS AND 1 MARYLAND).' THE CHART PRINTS SOMETHING ELSE. Scan p.143, read with eyes at 4x: '1941, Jan (GT 15...18). 44 x Hurricane I, 12 x Blenheim I, 1 x Wellington I. WD: 1F, 2B**.' That is 57 aeroplanes, one Wellington and NO Maryland. APPLIED: the chart's 57, which is what this file transcribes -- the example is prose illustrating how to divide a month amongst its weeks, the chart is the schedule itself, and 34.86 is the case that points the player at the chart. The discrepancy is two aeroplanes on one row of twenty-eight and moves nothing measurable; it is recorded because an unrecorded book-internal disagreement is how a transcription quietly becomes a design.
_key_34_86: The chart's own Key, VERBATIM (PDF p.143, read with eyes): "Entries are the number of that type of plane that is received by the Commonwealth Player during that month, unless a particular Game-Turn is specified, divided as evenly as possible amongst the four weeks. WD: Squadron withdrawals, the number of each type of squadron that must be removed from play at the beginning of the month indicated (except Nov II, 1940), if any. The squadron must consist of at least 12 planes and may be removed from anywhere on the game maps. Squadron removals may come from Malta and/or the Allied Off-Map airfields. F: Any squadron consisting of at least 12 planes capable of Combat Air Patrol. B: Any squadron consisting of at least 12 planes capable of performing bombing missions. R: Any squadron of at least 12 planes capable of performing reconnaissance missions. *One squadron must consist of at least 12 Beaufighters (any combination of the two models). †Both squadrons must each consist of at least 12 Beaufighters (any combination of the two models). ‡One squadron must consist of at least 9 Bomb Points (any combination of planes with 9 or more Bomb Points). **Two squadrons must each consist of at least 12 planes with a possible Bombload Capacity of at least 9 Bomb Points (any combination of planes with 9 or more Bomb Points)."
_rule_34_81: [34.81] VERBATIM, and it is the whole of what the engine applies from this schedule: "The Commonwealth Player's airforce reinforcements may be placed in any air facility in Malta, Cairo, Alexandria, or Off-map except Ethiopia. The reinforcements may be divided amongst the receiving air facilities in any distribution the Commonwealth Player chooses except as follows: A/No more than 10% of a month's airplane reinforcements may be sent to Malta. B/No airplane reinforcements may be sent to a Malta/N African Off-map air facility in excess of the facility's current squadron capacity." So the SCHEDULE is a law and the DIVISION is a free choice under two caps. game.malta.reinforcement applies both caps; what it sends under them is OUR ASSIGNMENT of the free choice and is argued there and at _our_assignment below.
_rule_34_84: [34.84] VERBATIM (the arrival cadence): "Airplane reinforcements arrive in the Naval Convoy Arrival Phase. The reinforcements are listed as to the total of each type of plane arriving during that month (the beginning of the campaign has arrivals during certain specific gameturns instead of months). The planes must be divided amongst the weeks as evenly as possible and all of the planes arriving during a game-turn may arrive in the First Op Stage of that game-turn at the owning Player's choice." Hence `turns` on every row -- the four Game-Turns of the month, or the single Game-Turn the 1940 rows name -- and hence the even division game.malta.reinforcement makes across them.

torpedo_types_4_44a._rule: [4.44A] chart key, verbatim: 'Bomb = Bombload Capacity AND TORPEDO CARRYING CAPABILITY, IF ANY, in points. "-" = May not carry bombs. ONLY PLANES POSSESSING AN "/T" MAY CARRY TORPEDOS. The number to the left of the stroke is the Bombload Capacity. The number to the right of the T is the Torpedo Capacity.' THE WHOLE COMMONWEALTH AIRCRAFT CHART CARRIES AN /T ON EXACTLY THREE ROWS, and all three at the same Torpedo Capacity -- read with eyes off the scan at 400 dpi, both tables end to end (PDF p.112 Fighters & Fighter-Bombers: NO /T anywhere; PDF p.113 Bombers, Flying Boats, Transports, Reconnaissance): Albacore '8/T8', Beaufort Mk. I '-/T8', Swordfish Mk. I '-/T8'. Nothing else on either table may carry a torpedo at all.
torpedo_types_4_44a._why_the_engine_reads_it: [41.73] is a condition, not a constant: 'When Commonwealth planes fly against Axis convoys and AT LEAST 50% OF THE PLANES ARE CARRYING TORPEDOES, in determining the level (Case 41.66), increase the bombing tonnage by 25%.' Malta's Bomb Points are the Torpedo Capacity of its torpedo aircraft counted as bombs (41.74) plus that 25%, so the island's anti-shipping BUCKET admits torpedo aircraft and nothing else -- see game.malta.strike_establishment. That the three /T rows share one Torpedo Capacity is what lets the bucket stay a single count instead of a per-type ledger: every aeroplane in it is worth the 8 that data/malta_44.json initial_setup_60_46.strike_weapon prices it at, and the 50% test is satisfied by construction rather than assumed.
torpedo_types_4_44a._acceptance: THE BOOK'S OWN LATER SCENARIOS PRINT THIS BUCKET AND THEY ARE THE ACCEPTANCE TEST: [60.46] 12 Swordfish, [61.34] 10 Swordfish, [62.36] 3 Swordfish, [63.37] 3 Swordfish and 3 Albacores. Malta's torpedo arm SHRINKS through the war in the book -- which is why the pro-rata composition below is the faithful reading and 'send Malta the torpedo aircraft first' is not: this schedule musters 116 torpedo aeroplanes against 6,691 in all (1.7%), so a tenth of a month taken pro rata, floored, is zero in twelve of the sixteen months that carry any and one or two in the rest -- six aeroplanes over the whole war, and on the measured campaign the island is at its planned establishment on each of those months so none of the six lands either. Malta's torpedo arm therefore only ever decays under 41.36, which is the direction the four printed snapshots run. The full argument, including what the under-read costs, is at game.malta.reinforcement.

malta_share_34_81a._rule: [34.81] A/ 'No more than 10% of a month's airplane reinforcements may be sent to Malta.' The magnitude, held here rather than as a literal in game.malta. It is a CEILING, so the engine rounds the share DOWN.

_our_assignment: OUR ASSIGNMENT OF 34.81'S FREE CHOICE, STATED IN FULL BECAUSE IT IS A CHOICE AND NOT A TRANSCRIPTION -- AND REBUILT 2026-07-22 ON THE BOOK'S OWN NUMBERS AFTER THE FIRST VERSION TURNED OUT TO BE A NO-OP. 34.81 makes the SCHEDULE a law and the DIVISION a free choice under two caps, and the free choice has two halves: how many aeroplanes the Commonwealth sends Malta, and how many Capacity Levels he builds there (44.13's construction table is a 'may', metered by nothing in the book but his judgement). THE FIRST VERSION COUPLED THE TWO -- capacity built to hold the aeroplanes, aeroplanes admitted as far as capacity held them -- and the two halves capped each other into a FIXED POINT at exactly the invented five-level ceiling it was meant to replace: planes could never exceed 18 x capacity, so ceil(planes / 18) could never exceed capacity, so the ceiling never rose. Malta stood at 5 levels and 90 aeroplanes for the whole war, against the book's 8 levels by March 1941 and 28 by October 1942, and 44.13 was dead code presented as a live mechanism. WHAT REPLACES IT USES ONLY PRINTED NUMBERS: THE BOOK'S OWN FOUR SNAPSHOTS OF MALTA ARE THE COMMONWEALTH'S PLAN. [60.46] September 1940: 5 Capacity Levels, 31 aeroplanes, 12 of them torpedo-armed. [61.34] March 1941: 8 levels, 55 aeroplanes, 10 torpedo. [62.36] November 1941: 14 levels, 74 aeroplanes, 3 torpedo. [63.37] October 1942: all facilities at full capacity (28 levels), 118 aeroplanes, 6 torpedo. Those are recorded in data/malta_44.json later_scenario_establishments and they are now READ, not merely filed: the Commonwealth builds Malta's capacity toward the levels the book prints for the current date (game.malta.repair_ceiling, still one [44.5] die per facility per Game-Turn, so the island climbs and an Axis raid knocks it back), and sends it aeroplanes up to the establishment the book prints for the current date (game.malta.reinforcement), under 34.81A's 10% ceiling and 34.81B's capacity cap, both of which still bind. Between two snapshots the earlier one stands: a step function of printed values, with no interpolation, because interpolating would be inventing a number the book does not print. WHAT THIS PRODUCES is the book's own shape by construction -- Malta grows 5 -> 8 -> 14 -> 28 levels and 31 -> 55 -> 74 -> 118 aeroplanes, reached FROM BELOW at the rate the schedule and the construction die allow, and knocked back down by every raid that lands. THE COMPOSITION IS NOT ASSIGNED: it is pro rata over the month's own printed types (see reinforcement), which is what makes the torpedo arm shrink toward the 10 / 3 / 6 the later scenarios print rather than being steered there. WHAT IS STILL OURS AND NOT THE BOOK'S: that the Commonwealth aims at his own historical establishment at all. He is free under 34.81 to send Malta nothing, or to send it 34.81A's whole tenth (669 aeroplanes over the campaign, onto an island the book never shows holding more than 118). Both extremes are legal and both are absurd; aiming at the four printed snapshots is the only assignment available that needs no number the book does not print. It belongs to a Commonwealth air seat this engine does not yet have.
_what_is_not_wired: TWO THIRDS OF THIS CHART IS TRANSCRIBED AND UNAPPLIED, DELIBERATELY. (1) THE MAINLAND NINETY PER CENT. 34.81 divides a month's arrivals between Malta and the mainland Commonwealth air force, and only the Malta branch is wired; state.air's Commonwealth wings do not grow. That is not an oversight -- [34.87] AXIS AIRPLANE REINFORCEMENT SCHEDULE IS STILL UNTRANSCRIBED, so growing the Royal Air Force by six thousand aeroplanes while the Regia Aeronautica stays at [60.32]'s 385 would be a one-sided invention several times larger than anything Malta does. The two schedules land together or neither does. (2) THE WITHDRAWAL COLUMN. 34.85 requires the Commonwealth to withdraw the charted squadrons at the beginning of the month indicated, and the Key says they 'may be removed from anywhere on the game maps... may come from Malta and/or the Allied Off-Map airfields' -- MAY, not must. With no mainland ledger to remove a squadron from, applying WD to Malta alone would take the withdrawals entirely out of the island, which is the one place the chart does not oblige him to take them. The column is transcribed on every row and nothing reads it; it comes alive with the mainland half.
_role_assignment: THE `role` COLUMN IS OURS, and it is assigned exactly as data/air_establishments.json assigns it -- off the [4.44A] chart, by WHICH OF ITS TWO TABLES the type is printed in and by the Mission Capability cell on its row (both read with eyes, PDF p.112 Fighters & Fighter-Bombers, p.113 Bombers, Flying Boats, Transports, Reconnaissance). A type on the fighters table takes `fighters` (the Skua Mk. II included -- the chart's own note calls it a divebomber, but it is printed on the fighters table and 34.21's pilot requirement follows the table). On the bombers table, a row whose capability cell carries B takes `strike`, a row carrying only R takes `recon`, and the Anson Mk. I -- R and B with a Bombload of 2 -- goes to `recon` on the same reading that sends the Ca 309 Ghibli there, while the 167 Maryland -- R and B at a Bombload of 9 -- goes to `strike` on the same reading that sends the S.M. 81 Pipistrello there. The A-28 Hudson carries NO D/R/B cell at all, only a transport tonnage, so it is `transport` -- a fourth bucket game.state.AirWing does not have, counted in Malta's establishment (41.36's bombs fall on 'the planes on the ground' without regard to type) and in nothing else.

commonwealth_34_86._comment: One row per printed entry, in the chart's own order. `turns` is the Game-Turn or Game-Turns the row names; `total` is the row's plane count (the arithmetic sum of `planes`, carried so a transcription slip fails a test rather than a campaign); `withdrawals` is the WD column verbatim, unapplied.
